title: CSS oriented to objects (OOCSS)
body:
## WEBCAT

Yesterday I attended the [webcat of September](http://lanyrd.com/2011/webcat-september/ "webcat september 2011"), a **monthly event in Barcelona aimed at web designers and programmers** where they themselves propose a topic during the month and present it that day before all attendees, with the only requirement that the presentation does not exceed 10 minutes. 10 minutes are few, but they condense a lot since the presentations are usually very prepared and dynamic, and after them there is a question round that enriches much more what was presented.

## OOCSS

## ![OOCSS](http://www.xarop.com/wp-content/uploads/2011/09/oocss1-300x174.gif "OOCSS Object Oriented CSS")

Among other very interesting topics (Drupal distributions, agile development, typography and css, ...) [Harold Dennison](http://hdennison.com "hdennison.com/") gave us an interesting introduction to **[OOCSS](https://github.com/stubbornella/oocss/wiki/ "GitHub")** (Object Oriented CSS), object-oriented CSS. [Nicole Sullivan](http://www.stubbornella.org "stubbornella.org") is the promoter of this "philosophy" that is initially **designed to optimize code** and save thousands of lines and therefore Kb and loading and response times for large websites like Yahoo or Amazon. In short, the practice is about optimizing CSS code and reusing it. We usually tend to focus this optimization on HTML code, we like it simple, clean and with as few lines as possible and this usually generates more complex CSS and generally what we gain in Kb in HTML we lose in CSS.

### Reuse

We usually create CSS following the HTML semantics of the site, and we create styles associated with the various HTML tags. OOCSS proposes **separating structure (HTML) from *skin* (CSS)** and using and reusing classes to the maximum. For example instead of defining h2 {font-size:2em;} use a class .big{font-size:2em;} and apply it <h2 class="big">. At first it may seem like more code, but if we reuse this class in other parts of our *site* we can save lines. It may be that for very simple sites we don't gain much, or we even complicate the HTML a bit, but I think it is a technique that will help us better structure our designs. For more information you can visit Nicole's website, [http://www.stubbornella.org](http://www.stubbornella.org/), where you will find advice, tips and presentations like this:

**[Object Oriented CSS](http://www.slideshare.net/stubbornella/object-oriented-css "Object Oriented CSS")**

View more [presentations](http://www.slideshare.net/) from [Nicole Sullivan](http://www.slideshare.net/stubbornella)

   **Do you think it is a good methodology?**
